english: Milk tea with pearls less sugar

allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of pearl milk tea in Singapore is one medium cup of approximately 500 ml, equivalent to roughly 500 g, containing brewed black tea, non-dairy creamer, reduced sugar syrup, and tapioca pearls.

nutrient_source.title: Pearl Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pearl Milk Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010091. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pearl milk tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on analogous composition: black tea base (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate contribution), tapioca pearls (GI ~56–70 for cooked tapioca per University of Sydney GI Database), and reduced sugar syrup (sucrose GI ~65). Weighted blend of tapioca starch and sucrose components yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 53. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (GI 53 × carbohydrate_g per serving 44.1 g) / 100 = 23.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar (5.92 g/100 g) in a sweetened prepared beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; fibre <1 g/100 g and potassium data unavailable provide no mitigating benefit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid pearl milk tea including 'less sugar' variants, as sugar-sweetened beverages independently elevate blood pressure risk (AHA 2018); choose plain unsweetened black or green tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.92 g added sugar per 100 g and high-GI tapioca pearls (estimated composite GI 53, GL 23.4 per serving); dietary fibre is negligible at 0.03 g/100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pearl milk tea (less sugar) is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because it is a sugar-sweetened beverage with high-GI tapioca pearls and negligible fibre, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; plain unsweetened tea without pearls is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 1.2 g/100 g from palm oil-based non-dairy creamer, combined with added sugar (5.92 g/100 g); regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit pearl milk tea due to its saturated fat content from non-dairy creamer and added sugar, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased CVD risk; choose plain unsweetened tea without creamer.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory components: added sugar (5.92 g/100 g), palm oil-based non-dairy creamer (saturated fat ~1.2 g/100 g), and high-GI tapioca pearls; no qualifying anti-inflammatory factor present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pearl milk tea (less sugar) is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory combination of added sugar, saturated fat from palm oil-based creamer, and refined starch from tapioca pearls; plain unsweetened black tea provides polyphenol benefit without these additives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pearl Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian sweetened beverage consisting of brewed black tea combined with non-dairy creamer (typically palm oil-based), a reduced quantity of sugar syrup, and chewy tapioca pearls (also called boba or pearls). The drink is typically served cold or at room temperature in a sealed cup with a wide straw to accommodate the pearls. The 'less sugar' designation indicates a reduced sugar level compared to the standard preparation, typically around 30–50% less sugar, though the beverage still contains approximately 5.92 g of sugar per 100 g. The texture is creamy from the non-dairy creamer, mildly sweet, with a tea flavour and the characteristic chewy bite of the tapioca pearls. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 51 kcal, 0.24 g protein, 1.77 g fat (of which approximately 1.2 g is saturated fat from palm oil-based creamer), 8.82 g carbohydrate, 5.92 g sugar, 0.03 g dietary fibre, and 9 mg sodium. The estimated composite glycemic index is approximately 53 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 23.4 per standard 500 g serving. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its sugar content, high-GI tapioca pearls, and pro-inflammatory saturated fat from non-dairy creamer. It carries moderate cardiovascular concern due to saturated fat and added sugar. Despite the 'less sugar' label, it remains a sugar-sweetened beverage and should not be considered a healthy option for metabolic or inflammatory conditions.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010091) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for tapioca and sucrose, clearly documented. Saturated fat estimated from known non-dairy creamer composition consistent with HPB data context.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific pearl milk tea (less sugar) preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010091 or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite beverage as of 2026. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.2 g/100 g based on known palm oil-based non-dairy creamer composition consistent with HPB data context; this is an approximation and should be verified against direct laboratory analysis. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for pearl milk tea (less sugar) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input preserved as contextually accurate (sodium 9 mg/100 g; energy 51 kcal/100 g) but these tags do not imply suitability for any clinical condition given the sugar and saturated fat content.
